request.question: What is the setting for the game engine Snowdrop?

title: List of game engine recreations
Game engine
Id Tech 4
Unreal Engine
Rockstar Advanced Game Engine
Amazon Lumberyard
Snowdrop (game engine)
Game engine recreation
Tom Clancy's The Division
Zero (game engine)
sentences: Game engine recreation is a denomination for game engines that are clones of the original game engine binary that came with the video games.
The main motivation behind such a project is the wish to play the game on other operating systems than those supported by the original game engine.
A game engine is a software framework designed for the creation and development of video games. Developers use them to create games for consoles, mobile devices and personal computers.

The core functionality typically provided by a game engine includes a rendering engine ("renderer") for 2D or 3D graphics, a physics engine or collision detection (and collision response), sound, scripting, animation, artificial intelligence, networking, streaming, memory management, threading, localization support, scene graph, and may include video support for cinematics.
The process of game development is often economized, in large part, by reusing/adapting the same game engine to create different games, or to make it easier to port games to multiple platforms.
id Tech 4, popularly known as the "Doom 3" engine, is a game engine developed by id Software and first used in the video game "Doom 3".
The engine was designed by John Carmack.
Carmack also created previous game engines, such as those for Doom and Quake, which are widely recognized as significant advances in the field.
This OpenGL-based game engine has also been used in "Quake 4", "Prey", "", "Wolfenstein", and "Brink".
The Unreal Engine is a game engine developed by Epic Games, first showcased in the 1998 first-person shooter game "Unreal".

Although primarily developed for first-person shooters, it has been successfully used in a variety of other genres, including stealth, MMORPGs, and other RPGs. With its code written in C++, the Unreal Engine features a high degree of portability and is a tool used by many game developers today.
It has won several awards, including the Guinness World Records award for "most successful video game engine."
The Rockstar Advanced Game Engine (RAGE) is a proprietary game engine developed by the RAGE Technology Group at Rockstar San Diego, with contributions by other Rockstar Games subsidiaries.
The engine has been used on several different platforms such as Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 3, PlayStation 4, Wii, Xbox 360 and Xbox One.
The Angel Game Engine was originally developed by Angel Studios for "Midtown Madness" and later the sixth generation console era versions of the "Midnight Club" series and other Angel Studios games.
Amazon Lumberyard is a free cross-platform triple-A game engine developed by Amazon and based on the architecture of CryEngine, which was licensed from Crytek in 2015.
The engine features integration with Amazon Web Services to allow developers to build or host their games on Amazon's servers, as well as support for livestreaming via Twitch.

Additionally, the engine includes Twitch ChatPlay, where viewers of the Twitch stream can influence the game through the associated chat, a method of play inspired by the "Twitch Plays Pokémon" phenomenon.
The source code is available to end users with limitations: users may not publicly release the Lumberyard engine source code or use it to release their own game engine.

Lumberyard launched on February 9, 2016 alongside GameLift, a fee-based managed service for deploying and hosting multiplayer games, intended to allow developers to easily develop games that attract "large and vibrant communities of fans."

s of February 2017 , the software is currently in beta and can be used to build games for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 4, Xbox One, with limited support for iOS and Android, and support for Linux and Mac coming as well.
Virtual reality integration was added in Beta 1.3, allowing developers to build games supporting devices like Oculus Rift and HTC Vive.
Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One.
It was first revealed at E3 2013 with "Tom Clancy's The Division", the first game using the engine.
Game engine recreations are remade engine interpreters for video games that replace the original engine binary that came with the original game.

A notable example of game engine recreation is ScummVM which successfully recreated the SCUMM engine of classical LucasArts' point and click adventures. For further examples, refer to the list of game engine recreations.

Tom Clancy's The Division is an online-only action role-playing video game developed by Massive Entertainment and published by Ubisoft, with assistance from Red Storm Entertainment, for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 4 and Xbox One.
It was announced during Ubisoft's E3 2013 press conference, and was released worldwide on 8 March 2016.

It is set in a near future New York City in the aftermath of a smallpox pandemic; the player, who is an agent of the eponymous Strategic Homeland Division, commonly referred to as simply "The Division", is tasked with helping the group rebuild its operations in Manhattan, investigate the nature of the outbreak, and combating criminal activity in its wake.
"The Division" is structured with elements of role-playing games, as well as collaborative and player versus player online multiplayer.
Zero is a proprietary game engine created by Pandemic Studios.
It was used first in the game "" and later used in several "Star Wars" games including the popular "Battlefront" series.
"Battlezone II: Combat Commander" and "Dark Reign 2" both feature an in-engine editor accessible via commands.
The engine was revamped for "" to accommodate consoles and third person gameplay.
The engine was again retooled for "" and the level editor was made a separate entity from the game engine.
A set of modding tools including ZeroEdit, the new level creation tool, were released for use with "Star Wars: Battlefront" on December 23, 2004.
An updated version of the tools were released on February 2, 2006 for "".
Pandemic used Zero as their primary engine for several of their games developed in their Los Angeles, California studio.
A modified version of the Zero engine was also used in Pandemic's "Mercenaries 2: World in Flames".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What is the setting for the game engine Snowdrop?
hop1_docs: Snowdrop (game engine) | Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One.  It was first revealed at E3 2013 with "Tom Clancy's The Division", the first game using the engine.

Information set (game theory) | In game theory, an information set is a set that, for a particular player, establishes all the possible moves that could have taken place in the game so far, given what that player has observed.  If the game has perfect information, every information set contains only one member, namely the point actually reached at that stage of the game.  Otherwise, it is the case that some players cannot be sure exactly what has taken place so far in the game and what their position is.

List of snowdrop gardens | Snowdrops (species of "Galanthus") are popular late winter or early spring flowers which are celebrated as a sign of spring and can attract large numbers of visitors to places where they are growing.  The reason for their popularity is that snowdrops can form impressive carpets of white in areas where they are native or have been naturalised.  Most 'Snowdrop Gardens' will have the common snowdrop, "Galanthus nivalis", but some have more unusual snowdrops, some which may be unique to the garden that they are growing in.  The rarest may only survive in that garden due to the conditions and environment.
HMS Snowdrop | Two ships of the Royal Navy have borne the name HMS "Snowdrop" after the flower, the Snowdrop:

Galanthus nivalis | Galanthus nivalis, the snowdrop or common snowdrop, is the best-known and most widespread of the 20 species in its genus, "Galanthus".  Snowdrops are among the first bulbs to bloom in spring and can form impressive carpets of white in areas where they are native or have been naturalised.  They should not be confused with the snowflakes, in the genera "Leucojum" and "Acis".

USS Snowdrop (1863) | USS "Snowdrop", originally named "Albert DeGroat" and occasionally referred to as "A. DeGroat" was a screw tug built in 1863 at Buffalo, New York.  "Albert DeGroat" was purchased by the United States Navy at New York City on 16 October 1863; renamed "Snowdrop"; and was fitted out at the New York Navy Yard.
HMS Snowdrop (1915) | HMS "Snowdrop" was an "Azalea"-class sloop of the Royal Navy.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Snowdrop (game engine): quotes — "for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One" and "first revealed at E3 2013 with 'Tom Clancy's The Division'" (passage [1]).
- Ambiguities:
  - The term "setting" is ambiguous: it could mean (A) the engine's target platforms, (B) the narrative setting of a game that uses the engine, or (C) an engine configuration/technical setting. Only (A) is directly evidenced in the passages. No passage gives a narrative setting.
  - Only one Snowdrop page appears in the passages ([1]); a dedicated page for "Tom Clancy's The Division" is not included.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity: "Tom Clancy's The Division" (exactly as in passage [1]).
  - Missing fact needed: the specific meaning of "setting" intended by the question. If they mean narrative setting, the precise in-game setting (location/time/plot context) of "Tom Clancy's The Division" is missing from the provided passages.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Fetch the "Tom Clancy's The Division" (video game) page — check the lede/infobox and the "Plot" or "Setting" sections for the game's narrative location/time (example search: "Tom Clancy's The Division setting New York plot").
  2) Re-open "Snowdrop (game engine)" page — check lede/infobox and any "Platforms" or "Games using Snowdrop" section to confirm platform/usage details and list of games using the engine.
  3) If multiple games named "The Division" or variants exist, fetch the specific video game page(s) (e.g., disambiguation or "Tom Clancy's The Division (video game)") and check lede/infobox for setting.
Final answer withheld until those pages/sections are retrieved.
response.hop2_query: Tom Clancy's The Division setting New York plot
hop2_docs: Tom Clancy's The Division | Tom Clancy's The Division is an online-only action role-playing video game developed by Massive Entertainment and published by Ubisoft, with assistance from Red Storm Entertainment, for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 4 and Xbox One.  It was announced during Ubisoft's E3 2013 press conference, and was released worldwide on 8 March 2016.  It is set in a near future New York City in the aftermath of a smallpox pandemic; the player, who is an agent of the eponymous Strategic Homeland Division, commonly referred to as simply "The Division", is tasked with helping the group rebuild its operations in Manhattan, investigate the nature of the outbreak, and combating criminal activity in its wake.  "The Division" is structured with elements of role-playing games, as well as collaborative and player versus player online multiplayer.
Red Rabbit | Red Rabbit (2002) is a bestselling novel by Tom Clancy.  It incorporates the 1981 plot to assassinate Pope John Paul II.  It made it on to the "New York Times" bestseller list.

Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon: Jungle Storm | Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon: Jungle Storm is an expansion of "Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon" released on the PlayStation 2.  It contains the content of "" along with eight new single-player missions set in Colombia and additional multiplayer maps.

Tom Clancy's Net Force | Tom Clancy's Net Force is a novel series, created by Tom Clancy and Steve Pieczenik and originally written by Steve Perry.  The original series ceased publication in 2006.  Relaunched in 2013, and currently written by veteran Tom Clancy author Jerome Preisler, it is set in 2018 and charts the actions of Net Force: a special executive branch of the United States government set up to combat increasing crime and terrorist activity on the Internet.  There was also a spin-off of young adult books called "Net Force Explorers".

Without Remorse | Without Remorse is a thriller novel published in 1993 by Tom Clancy and is a part of the Jack Ryan universe series.  While not the first novel of the series to be published, it is first in plot chronology.  The main setting of the book is set during the Vietnam War, in the American city of Baltimore.  The book focuses on the development of one of Clancy's recurring characters, John Kelly/John Clark, while providing the character some back-story.  The book serves to give a history of Kelly's life, and explains how he becomes John Clark.

Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six: Shadow Vanguard | Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six: Shadow Vanguard is a 2011 first-person shooter developed and published by Gameloft for iOS, Xperia Play and Android devices.  The game is set within the continuity of "Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six" media franchise.

Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon 2: Summit Strike | Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon 2: Summit Strike is the expansion to "Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon 2".  There are several minor differences between "Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon 2: Summit Strike", and "Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon 2".  The most notable being the difficulty, Summit Strike being regarded as the harder of the two.  Other differences would include new multiplayer modes, such as Heli Hunt.
hop2_titles: Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon: Jungle Storm
Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six: Shadow Vanguard
Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon 2: Summit Strike
response.summary_2: "for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One" Tom Clancy's The Division is set in near-future New York City after a smallpox pandemic.
response.answer: The Division (which first showcased Snowdrop) is set in near-future New York City after a smallpox pandemic.
